title: Great Depression
description: Test your knowledge of the causes and effects of the Great Depression!

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Great Crash of October 1929 marked a fundamental break in U.S. history, a drastic change in basic attitudes and institutions that define the roles of citizen and state. . . [T]he public mind was affected as much as the economy, with the people turning to the government for security. . . 
> “The terror of the Great Crash has been the failure to explain it. People were left with the feeling that massive economic contractions could occur at any moment, without warning, without cause. That fear has been exploited ever since as the major justification for virtually unlimited federal intervention in economic affairs.”

>-Source: Alan Reynolds, economist, “Fifty Years Later: What Do We Know About the Great Crash?” *National Review,* 1979

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument about life after the Stock Market Crash of 1929?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People were concerned about another Great Depression and gave the federal government more power to regulate the economy.
People felt like too much intervention in the economy caused the Great Depression and wanted to return to a laissez-faire economic policy.
People were concerned that foreign trade would never recover and wanted to eliminate all tariffs to stimulate the economy.
People lost trust in the government’s ability to solve economic crises and wanted to decentralize the power of the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Great Crash of October 1929 marked a fundamental break in U.S. history, a drastic change in basic attitudes and institutions that define the roles of citizen and state. . . [T]he public mind was affected as much as the economy, with the people turning to the government for security. . . 
> “The terror of the Great Crash has been the failure to explain it. People were left with the feeling that massive economic contractions could occur at any moment, without warning, without cause. That fear has been exploited ever since as the major justification for virtually unlimited federal intervention in economic affairs.”

>-Source: Alan Reynolds, economist, “Fifty Years Later: What Do We Know About the Great Crash?” *National Review,* 1979

**Which of the following would best serve as an example of how the government intervened in economic affairs during the Great Depression?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the passage of the Smoot-Hawley Tariff
the ratification of the Sixteenth Amendment
the creation of the House Un-American Activities Committee
the introduction of the Neutrality Act

assessment_items.question_content:
>“The Great Crash of October 1929 marked a fundamental break in U.S. history, a drastic change in basic attitudes and institutions that define the roles of citizen and state. . . [T]he public mind was affected as much as the economy, with the people turning to the government for security. . . 
> “The terror of the Great Crash has been the failure to explain it. People were left with the feeling that massive economic contractions could occur at any moment, without warning, without cause. That fear has been exploited ever since as the major justification for virtually unlimited federal intervention in economic affairs.”

>-Source: Alan Reynolds, economist, “Fifty Years Later: What Do We Know About the Great Crash?” *National Review,* 1979

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that the Great Depression had on society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to calls for the federal government to have a more active role in regulating the economy.
It led to a rise in emigration from the United States to Canada and Europe where the economy was stronger.
It led to mass internal migration to the Sunbelt for farmers who were located in the Midwest.
It led to the federal government returning to a laissez-faire economic policy to address the failing economy.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “S.J. (Interviewer): Could you tell me a little bit about the dust storms? 

> “Criswell: Our cotton would be about five or six inches tall and it looked like a fire had gone over that field of cotton. It would just kill it and then in a few days it would be dead. . . I've seen it take out a whole big field of cotton just like that. We lived on the sandy land. The storms would start up in Kansas or somewhere. These local sand storms could sometimes could ruin your crop. . . They'd come just like a big old black cloud and they soon covered everything. . . A lot of the crops that were older it would just damage. You couldn't start over again. If the cotton was small enough you could start over again. It would just damage it so it wouldn't grow right.”

> Source: Vera Ruth Woodwall Criswell, Interview from the California Odyssey archive, 1981

**The phenomena described in the excerpt is an example of which of the following developments in the 1930s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Dust Bowl
Black Tuesday
Teapot Dome Scandal
Mount St. Helens eruption

These local sand storms could sometimes could ruin your crop. . . They'd come just like a big old black cloud and they soon covered everything. . . **Which of the following was the most immediate impact of conditions such as those described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: rise in internal migration from the plains to western states
introduction of legislative measures to increase crop production
creation of programs to hire former farmers for government jobs in the East
transition to an industrial economy as people left agriculture

> “S.J. (Interviewer): Could you tell me a little bit about the dust storms? 

> “Criswell: Our cotton would be about five or six inches tall and it looked like a fire had gone over that field of cotton. It would just kill it and then in a few days it would be dead. . . These local sand storms could sometimes could ruin your crop. . . They'd come just like a big old black cloud and they soon covered everything. . . **Which of the following United States government actions most directly resulted from the conditions described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The government instituted a program to deport people of Mexican descent.
The government began creating job training programs to transition people away from farming.
The government passed a series of tariffs to help combat the financial losses.
The government set immigration quotas to curb mass immigration to the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Dear Sir: 
> “I am persuaded to write you, concerning aid to unemployment. I hope this movement will be speeded up so people in Pottstown will feel and know the results before Cold weather comes upon us, the struggling starving working class under nourished [sic] Men[,] women, and children. It really is alarming that this so called [sic] prosperous Nation that we must suffer on [account] of a few men seeking power and rule and have laws pass to suit themselves. . . I am one of the men out of work but the rich dont [sic] care so long as they have full and plenty. . . 
> “I hope relief will be coming soon and some action not Just paper talk.”

>-Source: Author unknown from Pottstown, PA, Letter to Herbert Hoover, *Down and Out in the Great Depression: Letters from the Forgotten Man,* 1930 

**The excerpt reflects many Americans’ belief at the time that the federal government was: **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: failing to address the immediate effects of the Great Depression.
responding correctly and ensuring that the US economy will recover.
prioritizing the plights of farmers over factory workers.
worsening the depression by intervening in the economy.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Dear Sir: 
> “I am persuaded to write you, concerning aid to unemployment. I hope this movement will be speeded up so people in Pottstown will feel and know the results before Cold weather comes upon us, the struggling starving working class under nourished [sic] Men[,] women, and children. It really is alarming that this so called [sic] prosperous Nation that we must suffer on [account] of a few men seeking power and rule and have laws pass to suit themselves. . . I am one of the men out of work but the rich dont [sic] care so long as they have full and plenty. . . 
> “I hope relief will be coming soon and some action not Just paper talk.”

>-Source: Author unknown from Pottstown, PA, Letter to Herbert Hoover, *Down and Out in the Great Depression: Letters from the Forgotten Man,* 1930 

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how American views changed to call for the federal government to solve economic problems
how Americans lost trust in the federal government’s ability to solve economic and social issues
how Americans relocated to better land following the Great Depression and the Dust Bowl
how Americans pushed for the decentralization of the federal government to give state and local governments more power

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Dear Sir: 
> “I am persuaded to write you, concerning aid to unemployment. I hope this movement will be speeded up so people in Pottstown will feel and know the results before Cold weather comes upon us, the struggling starving working class under nourished [sic] Men[,] women, and children. It really is alarming that this so called [sic] prosperous Nation that we must suffer on [account] of a few men seeking power and rule and have laws pass to suit themselves. . . I am one of the men out of work but the rich dont [sic] care so long as they have full and plenty. . . 
> “I hope relief will be coming soon and some action not Just paper talk.”

>-Source: Author unknown from Pottstown, PA, Letter to Herbert Hoover, *Down and Out in the Great Depression: Letters from the Forgotten Man,* 1930 

**The author’s account in the excerpt above most directly reflects which of the following changes in the 1930s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the election of Franklin Roosevelt over Herbert Hoover
the introduction of medical care programs to support the health of poorer Americans
the creation of a work-abroad programs to offer job opportunities
the reduction of taxes for the top-earners to boost job creation

assessment_items.question_content:
> “. . . Could we not have employment and food to eat. . . Why does Every Thing have Exceptional Value. Except the Human being— why are we reduced to poverty and starving and anxiety and Sorrow So quickly under your administration as Chief Executor Can not you find a quicker way of Executing us than to Starve us to death. . . how can we be Law abiding citizens and educate our children and be Happy Content with nothing to do nothing to eat. When your System has Every Thing under control and cant [sic] use It. nor will you give any thing [sic] a way [sic]. Why take more than you need, why make Laws and allow industry to take It all.” 

>-Source: Author unknown from Vinland, NJ, Letter to Herbert Hoover, *Down and Out in the Great Depression: Letters from the Forgotten Man,* 1930 

**The author’s account in the excerpt above most directly encouraged which of the following changes in the 1930s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: introduction of laws and programs to help offer relief to citizens affected by the Great Depression
construction of settlement houses for people who were losing their homes due to unemployment
creation of programs to admit people of Mexican descent into the country to serve in jobs in the agricultural industry
reassignment of major factories to create weapons and other government property in order to hire unemployed people

**Situations such as those described in the excerpt were most directly addressed by which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: New Deal programs
Mexican Repatriation policy
Bracero program
Reaganomics policies

**The author’s ideas expressed in the excerpt emerged most directly in response to which of the following developments in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an episode of market instability that led to massive unemployment
a lack of jobs for African Americans following World War I
an economic policy that supported free trade over domestic production
a foreign policy that limited immigrants to the United States
